perturbation.original_text: The parties shall attempt, in an amicable manner, to settle any dispute which may arise under this agreement. If any such dispute cannot be resolved by negotiation, it sh all as far as legally permissible and in compliance with statutory provisions, referred to the exclusive jurisdiction of the court in Antwerp.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties shall attempt, in an amicable manner, to settle any dispute which may arise under this agreement. If any such dispute cannot be resolved by negotiation within 10 days, it shall as far as legally permissible and in compliance with statutory provisions, referred to the exclusive jurisdiction of the court in Antwerp.
perturbation.explanation: The introduction of a 10-day negotiation period before resorting to court jurisdiction may be inconsistent with Belgian law, which might allow parties to seek immediate legal recourse without a mandatory waiting period.
perturbation.law_citation: Art. 734 of the Belgian Judicial Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Art. 734 of the Belgian Judicial Code describes procedures to initiate legal proceedings. Introducing a mandatory negotiation period of 10 days might contradict the immediacy with which a party can bring a case before the court, depending on the specific nature of the dispute and applicable provisions. While amicable settlements are encouraged, they are not a mandatory precursor in all cases.
perturbation.location: 5. Governing law and dispute resolution II.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Judiciary of Belgium - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia (Redirected from Judicial system of Belgium ) Court system overview This article needs additional citations for verification . Please help improve this article by adding citations to reliable sources . Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. Find sources: "Judiciary of Belgium" – news · newspapers · books · scholar · JSTOR ( December 2013 ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) Politics of Belgium Constitution Taxation Constitutional Court Monarchy Monarch ( list ) Philippe Duchess of Brabant Elisabeth of Belgium Crown Council Royal Family Federal Parliament Senate President : Vincent Blondel Chamber of Representatives President : Peter De Roover Federal Cabinet De Wever Government Prime Minister : Bart De Wever Judiciary Administrative jurisdictions Council of State Highest court Court of Cassation Appellate courts Court of appeal Court of labour Trial courts Court of assizes Tribunal of first instance Civil tribunal Correctional tribunal Family and juvenile tribunal Sentence enforcement tribunal Labour tribunal Enterprise tribunal Minor crimes Minor claims Police tribunal Justice of the peace Recent elections European 2009 2014 2019 2024 Federal 2010 2014 2019 2024 Regional 2009 2014 2019 2024 Local 2006 2012 2018 2024 Political parties Subdivisions Communities and regions Provinces Arrondissements Municipalities Foreign relations France-Belgium relations Belgium-Germany relations Belgium-United Kingdom relations Belgium-Netherlands relations Belgium–Luxembourg relations Politics of the European Union Belgium portal Other countries v t e The judiciary of Belgium is similar to the French judiciary . Belgium evolved from a unitary to a federal state , but its judicial system has not been adapted to a federal system. The Belgian judiciary is referred to as the courts and tribunals ( Dutch : hoven en rechtbanken , French : cours et tribunaux , German : Gerichtshöfe und Gerichte ) in official texts, such as the Belgian Constitution . Normal judicial system [ edit ] Belgian judges leaving St Bavo's Cathedral in Ghent after the solemn Te Deum on the National Holiday Belgian judicial hierarchy as of 2020: One for the entire country Organised per judicial area Organised per province (+ Brussels) Organised per judicial arrondissement Organised per judicial canton Judicial subdivisions of the territory [ edit ] As of 2018, the territory of Belgium is subdivided into 5 judicial areas ( Antwerp , Brussels , Ghent , Liège and Mons ), 12 judicial arrondissements and 187 judicial cantons for the purpose of organising the judicial system. Before April 2014, when the judicial subdivisions were reformed into the current ones, there were 27 judicial arrondissements and 225 judicial cantons. Except for Brussels and the provinces of Flemish Brabant and Liège , the current judicial arrondissements correspond with the provinces of Belgium . Map of the 5 judicial areas (French names) Map of the 12 judicial arrondissements as of 2014 (French names) Map of the 27 judicial arrondissements from before 2014 Court of Cassation [ edit ] Main article: Court of Cassation (Belgium) The Court of Cassation ( Dutch : Hof van Cassatie , French : Cour de Cassation , German : Kassationshof ) is the supreme court of the Belgian judicial system. It only hears appeals in last resort against judgments and other decisions of lower courts (mostly the appellate courts ), and only points of law . This means the Court of Cassation will not review or reconsider the findings of fact established by lower courts. The jurisdiction of the Court is limited to either upholding a decision that is contested, or either annulling ( quashing ) the contested decision if the decision violated or misinterpreted the law. The latter is referred to as "cassation". In case of cassation, the Court of Cassation will generally refer the case to a different court of the same rank as the one who
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for immediate legal recourse, whereas the changed text introduces a mandatory 10-day negotiation period. The Law Explanation states that this may contradict the immediacy with which a party can bring a case before the court. The modified clause imposes a waiting period, potentially delaying legal action which would be in contradiction of the original clause which implies immediacy. The scraped text generally describes the court system of Belgium, and supports the notion that individuals have direct access to courts, implying that the changed text is an inconsistency

perturbation.original_text: This agreement is subject to Belgian law
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement is subject to Luxembourg law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Luxembourg law may create inconsistencies if the contract's provisions do not comply with Luxembourg law, especially regarding data protection or employment law issues, creating a conflict of laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Luxembourg Law of July 24, 2014 on combating money laundering and terrorist financing
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the governing law subjects the NDA to Luxembourg laws, such as the Law of July 24, 2014, on combating money laundering and terrorist financing. If the original agreement doesn't meet Luxembourg's compliance requirements in these areas, it introduces a conflict.
perturbation.location: 5. Governing law and dispute resolution I.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Belgian law, while the changed text specifies Luxembourg law. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that inconsistencies may arise if the contract's provisions do not comply with Luxembourg law. The change, therefore, introduces a legal contradiction under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because if the contract does not meet Luxembourg's compliance requirements, it becomes unenforceable under that legal system. The scraped snippets provided are insufficient to fully assess the Luxembourg legal implications, but the explanations strongly suggest a potential conflict of laws, which is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The terms of this agreement shall continue without limitation of time.
perturbation.changed_text: The terms of this agreement shall continue for a period of 1 year.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the duration of the confidentiality agreement to one year may not be sufficient to protect certain types of confidential information under Belgian law, depending on the nature of the information and the industry.
perturbation.law_citation: Belgian Act of 30 July 2018 on the protection of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The Belgian Act on Trade Secrets protects confidential information. A one-year limitation may be deemed insufficient for protecting trade secrets, leading to potential conflicts if the confidential information requires longer protection, as dictated by commercial reasonableness.

perturbation.justification: The original text ensures indefinite confidentiality. The changed text limits confidentiality to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that a one-year limitation on trade secret protection may be insufficient under Belgian law, creating a conflict if the information requires longer protection. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address trade secret protection duration, the Law Explanation argues that the one-year limitation may conflict with the Belgian Act on Trade Secrets, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Vedrova is entitled to equitable or injunction relief.
perturbation.changed_text: Vedrova is entitled to injunctive relief, provided Supplier is given a 30-day cure period following the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a mandatory 30-day cure period may contradict the immediate need for injunctive relief in cases where irreparable harm may occur if the confidential information is disclosed, which contradicts the equitable principles under Belgian law.
perturbation.law_citation: Art. 584 of the Belgian Code of Civil Procedure
perturbation.law_explanation: Art. 584 of the Belgian Code of Civil Procedure allows for urgent interim measures, including injunctive relief, when necessary to prevent imminent and serious harm. Mandating a 30-day cure period before obtaining an injunction could preclude Vedrova from obtaining timely relief when immediate action is crucial to prevent irreparable damage from a breach of confidentiality.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ELI - BELGIQUE Aide ELI (Identifiant européen de la législation) Désolé, la requête que vous avez formulée dans la fenêtre d'adressage http n'a pas trouvé de réponse dans nos banques de données. Veuillez adapter les éléments introduits dans la fenêtre d'adressage http. Vous avez utilisé dans votre adressage l'acronyme eli, c'est-à-dire l'adressage par identifiant européen de la législation (IEL ou en anglais ELI pour European legislation identifier). ELI (european legislation identifier - identifiant européen de la législation) se limite en ce qui concerne la Belgique à un URI (Uniform Resource Identifier - identifiant uniforme d'une ressource) qui produit un système de navigation permettant d'effectuer des requêtes directement à partir de la fenêtre d'adressage Http. Les requêtes peuvent aussi y être exécutées par des machines puisqu'un adressage http structuré et invariable d'un texte, peut être formulé avec précision. Eli pour la Belgique Pour la législation belge, les documents électroniques sont rendus accessibles par deux banques de données (le Moniteur belge et Justel) gérées par les services du Moniteur belge, journal officiel du Royaume de Belgique. Le Moniteur belge fait partie du Service public fédéral Justice. L'usage d'Eli vous permet de rechercher: La législation belge originale et officielle publiée dans le Moniteur belge sous format électronique depuis juin 1997. La législation consolidée officieuse établie par Justel. Toutefois, cette recherche ne se limite pas à la législation primaire, c'est-à-dire à celle votée par les parlements fédéraux, régionaux ou communautaires, et publiée au Moniteur belge par les exécutifs fédéraux, régionaux ou communautaires. Elle intègre aussi la recherche sur la législation secondaire c'est-à-dire les arrêtés d'exécution pris par les gouvernements fédéral, régionaux et communautaires ainsi que par les ministres chargés de l'exécution d'une législation primaire. Structure de ce type de recherche : Une partie fixe : Chaque recherche dans la fenêtre d'adressage commence invariablement par : http://www.ejustice.just.fgov.be/eli/ Une partie variable : La partie fixe de l'adressage (http://www.ejustice.just.fgov.be/eli/) est suivie d'une partie dont tous les éléments peuvent avoir des valeurs différentes (DT/AAAA/MM/DD/Identificateur unique/version du texte). La réunion de la partie fixe et de la partie variable forme : http://www.ejustice.just.fgov.be/eli/DT/AAAA/MM/JJ/Identificateur Unique/version du texte où : DT représente le type du document. Cet élément est obligatoire, en son absence, il y a renvoi à la version bilingue de cette page d'aide. Les types de document disponibles sont : CONSTITUTION LOI (Législation fédérale) DECRET (Législation régionale et communautaire, depuis 1972) ORDONNANCE (Législation régionale bruxelloise, depuis 1990) ARRETE (Législation secondaire de niveau fédéral, régional et communautaire telle que les arrêté royaux, les arrêtés des gouvernements régionaux ou communautaires, les arrêtés ministériels) AAAA représente l'année de promulgation du document. Cet élément est obligatoire, en son absence, il y a renvoi à la version de la page d'aide dans la langue du type de document (DT). MM représente le mois de promulgation du document. Cet élément est facultatif, mais devient obligatoire si l'on souhaite compléter l'adressage par le jour de promulgation du document (JJ). JJ représente le jour de promulgation du document. Cet élément est facultatif, mais devient obligatoire si l'on souhaite compléter l'adressage par l'identificateur unique. Identificateur Unique est un numéro unique donné au texte lors de sa publication originale. Ce numéro possède 10 positions numériques commençant par l'année de dépôt du texte pour publication suivi de son numéro de travail dans la chaîne de production du journal officiel. Cet élément est facultatif, mais devient obligatoire si l'on souhaite compléter l'adressage par la version du texte. Version du texte deux versions du t
perturbation.justification: The original text grants Vedrova the right to injunctive relief without conditions. The changed text introduces a 30-day cure period, which, as the Law Explanation indicates, could prevent Vedrova from obtaining timely relief to prevent irreparable damage, contradicting Art. 584 of the Belgian Code of Civil Procedure. The scraped snippet discusses Belgian legislation and access to legal documents, emphasizing the official nature of documents published in the Moniteur belge. While the snippet does not explicitly detail Art. 584, it establishes that Belgian law allows for direct queries and structured access to legal texts, indirectly supporting the relevance and accessibility of the referenced article. The modification therefore undermines the potential for immediate injunctive relief where it would otherwise be available, constituting a significant contradiction.
